source: Generating Alpha — Ep.56: career arc, semis, NVDA/Tesla, AI infra cycles
summary: Supply-demand framework applied to semiconductor inventories is a reliable early warning system for sector downturns
summary_extended: Baker describes how his first major investment call at Fidelity in February 2000 was built on a supply-demand framework applied to semiconductor inventories, which he argues is a reliable early warning system for sector downturns. He observed that inventories at both semiconductor manufacturers and their end customers had reached all-time highs, with finished goods at record levels, while semiconductor stocks simultaneously traded at unprecedented valuation multiples — a combination he viewed as a clear bearish signal. This framework proved correct as the dot-com bust followed, validating the approach as a core part of his investment process. Baker presents this as a generalizable methodology: monitoring days inventory and finished goods levels across the semiconductor supply chain can provide early warning of cyclical downturns before they are reflected in stock prices. The investment implication is process-oriented rather than stock-specific — investors should track semiconductor inventory data as a leading indicator for sector-wide positioning decisions.
detail: Baker's first major call at Fidelity (Feb 2000) was based on observing all-time high inventories at both semiconductor companies and their customers, with finished goods at record levels — while stocks traded at unprecedented multiples. This framework led to a correct bearish call ahead of the dot-com bust.
confidence_evidence: Described as a formative, validated experience with a specific successful outcome
quote_fragment: customer inventories and days inventory were at all-time highs...finished goods were at all-time high
